CHANGELOG.md:9:- `git-sync replicate` subcommand and `git-sync plan --mode replicate` for
client.go:19:// Client provides the public orchestration API for git-sync.
README.md:1:# git-sync
README.md:3:`git-sync` mirrors refs from a source remote to a target remote without creating a local checkout. It uses an in-memory `go-git` object store and talks smart HTTP directly:
README.md:15:`git-sync` fills that gap. It streams source packs directly into target `receive-pack` when it can, plans every action before pushing, and emits typed JSON for automation.
README.md:23:- `git-sync sync`: mirror source refs into the target
README.md:24:- `git-sync replicate`: overwrite target refs to match source via relay, and fail rather than materialize locally
README.md:26:`sync` automatically bootstraps an empty target, so the same command covers initial seeding and ongoing sync. To preview what would happen without pushing, run `git-sync plan` — it takes the same flags as `sync`, and `--mode replicate` previews a `replicate` run.
README.md:32:`git-sync` is also a Go library. Use `entire.io/entire/gitsync` for the stable embedding surface (`Probe`, `Plan`, `Sync`, `Replicate`, typed results, auth and HTTP injection). `entire.io/entire/gitsync/unstable` exposes advanced controls (`Bootstrap`, `Fetch`, batching knobs, heap measurement) and is not stable.

docs/architecture.md:3:`git-sync` is a remote-to-remote Git mirroring tool and library over smart HTTP.
docs/architecture.md:7:The point of `git-sync` is not that Git mirroring is impossible without it. The point is that the usual alternatives are awkward at the exact layer operators often need:
docs/architecture.md:13:`git-sync` is meant to be that missing middle layer:
docs/architecture.md:28:## When To Use `git-sync`
docs/architecture.md:30:`git-sync` is most useful when the main problem is moving Git data between remotes without turning that into a persistent local clone service.
docs/architecture.md:49:- `git-sync` is the lower-state, relay-first, operationally cheaper model
docs/architecture.md:165:  The source computes the pack, `git-sync` coordinates the transfer, and the target receives it directly. Large repositories are expected to stay viable primarily through bootstrap and incremental relay.
docs/architecture.md:167:  Once `git-sync` stops relaying and starts building a local push, it must hold the relevant Git objects in memory long enough to compute object closure and encode the outgoing pack.
docs/usage.md:3:This guide covers day-to-day `git-sync` CLI usage: commands, common examples, machine-readable output, auth, and protocol behavior. For product rationale and memory model details, see [architecture.md](architecture.md). For the wire protocol walkthrough, see [protocol.md](protocol.md).
docs/usage.md:9:- `git-sync sync`: mirror source refs into the target
docs/usage.md:10:- `git-sync replicate`: overwrite target refs to match source via relay, and fail rather than materialize locally
docs/usage.md:12:`sync` automatically bootstraps an empty target, so the same command covers initial seeding and ongoing sync. To preview what would happen without pushing, run `git-sync plan` — it takes the same flags as `sync`, and `--mode replicate` previews a `replicate` run.
docs/usage.md:14:Additional commands (`bootstrap`, `probe`, `fetch`) and advanced flags are available through `git-sync --help` and the unstable library surface. They are not part of the recommended public surface.
docs/usage.md:21:git-sync replicate \

docs/testing.md:53:`git-sync-bench` runs repeatable empty-target benchmarks against a source repository. It creates a fresh bare target for each run and reports wall-clock time plus internal `syncer` measurement data.

docs/protocol.md:3:This document is a code-grounded walkthrough of the Git wire protocol pieces that `git-sync` implements directly: smart HTTP transport, pkt-line framing, capability negotiation, sideband multiplexing, and the relay path that streams a source pack into target `receive-pack` without local materialization.
